question: Is there a formally approved Enterprise AI Strategy that defines the "Right to Win" and "Right to Play" for AI initiatives?
To what extent are AI governance objectives directly linked to organizational KPIs and executive performance reviews?
Is there a sustainable funding model for AI governance activities (e.g., impact assessments, auditing, training) that scales with adoption?
How frequently is the ROI of the AI Governance framework reviewed to ensure it is enabling rather than hindering business velocity?
Does the governance framework include a specific process for monitoring and adapting to changes in local (ANZ) and international AI regulations?
How effectively does the organization manage "Exit Strategies" or decommissioning plans for AI systems that no longer meet ethical or performance standards?
Is there a standardized "Risk Tiering" methodology applied to all AI use cases to determine the depth of governance required?
How well are the internal AI policies aligned to recognized standards like NIST AI RMF or ISO/IEC 42001?
Has the organization defined quantitative "Fairness Thresholds" that must be met before a high-risk AI system is approved for production?
Is there a mandatory requirement for an Ethical Impact Assessment (EIA) for any AI system that interacts with protected demographic data?
Is there a centralized "AI System Registry" that captures the rationale, data sources, and approval history for every deployed model?
How easily can the organization provide an audit trail of "Who approved what and why" for a specific AI system's deployment?
Is AI governance integrated directly into the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) as an automated gate rather than a manual checklist?
How well do the Legal, Risk, and Engineering teams communicate regarding the technical trade-offs of compliance decisions?
To what degree does the organization have a real-time view of all "Shadow AI" or unsanctioned AI tool usage across the enterprise?
Are governance metrics (e.g., % of systems with completed risk assessments) regularly reported to the Board or Executive Committee?
Is there a clear internal policy for disclosing the use of AI to employees, and is this disclosure consistently applied?
Does the organization provide clear, plain-language explanations to end-users (clients) when AI is used to make or influence decisions?
Is there a clearly defined RACI matrix that identifies the single point of accountability for AI safety and ethical outcomes?
Does the Practice Director or CISO have the formal "Veto Power" to stop an AI project on ethical or risk grounds?
How are "Third-Party AI Vendors" held accountable for the safety and transparency of the models they provide?
How well do your internal governance policies translate across different business units or international subsidiaries?
Is the governance documentation (e.g., risk assessments) stored in a machine-readable format that can be ingested by other compliance tools?
Do developers view the AI governance process as a helpful guide that reduces their personal and professional liability?
Has the organization created "Fast-Path" governance tracks for low-risk use cases (e.g., internal productivity tools)?
Is there a governance requirement for a "Privacy Impact Assessment" (PIA) for any AI system processing personal or sensitive data?
Does governance mandate the use of centralized, approved "Clean Rooms" or sandboxes for experimenting with sensitive datasets?
How effectively does the governance posture change based on the specific context (e.g., internal vs. public-facing) of the AI application?
Does the governance framework require a re-assessment whenever an AI system's deployment context changes significantly?
Is there a formally documented "AI Incident Response Plan" that is distinct from standard IT incident response?
Does governance require a "Human-in-the-Loop" for all AI decisions classified as "High Impact" under your risk tiering?
Are there clear "Prohibited Use Cases" (e.g., social scoring, facial recognition in certain contexts) defined within the policy?
How frequently are "Post-Market Surveillance" reviews conducted to ensure AI systems remain safe after deployment?
Is there a formal mechanism for employees or external users to "Whistleblow" or report concerns regarding AI safety or ethics?
Does the organization conduct regular "Simulated AI Crisis" workshops to test governance and communication under pressure?
To what extent is your observability strategy explicitly documented and connected to your organisation's AI risk priorities?
How well does observability coverage align to business-critical AI systems — are your highest-risk systems the most thoroughly monitored?
How proportionate and sustainable is the operational burden of your observability infrastructure — is it maintained without excessive manual effort?
To what extent are inference costs, model selection, and resource consumption actively optimised within your observability and operations practice?
How reliably does your monitoring system itself remain operational when the AI systems it monitors experience failures or degradation?
How systematically are your AI systems tested against edge cases, stress scenarios, and worst-case conditions to establish resilience baselines?
How thoroughly are data quality issues, preprocessing risks, and representativeness gaps identified and tracked across your AI data pipelines?
How well-defined and enforced are your AI model testing and validation processes — covering accuracy, robustness, and behaviour across intended use cases?
How effectively does drift detection identify changes in data distributions or model behaviour that impact accuracy or safety?
How clearly are human oversight checkpoints mapped across the AI lifecycle — and are they consistently enforced at decision points requiring human judgment?
To what extent does your observability framework actively measure and surface bias, fairness issues, and disparate performance across sub-populations?
How well does your monitoring detect harmful output patterns — such as performance degradation, discriminatory results, or outputs that violate stated guardrails?
Are mechanisms in place for users or stakeholders to contest or report problematic AI outputs — and are those reports tracked and acted upon?
How structured and complete is your prompt and inference logging — can you reconstruct model inputs, outputs, and reasoning at any point in time for audit purposes?
How well are explainability and interpretability characteristics measured and reported — proportionate to system risk and the needs of the audience reviewing them?
How completely does your observability cover all application layers — from data ingestion and model training through to inference outputs and downstream systems?
How well is data lineage tracked from source data through model versions to final outputs — enabling traceability of any given result back to its origin?
How comprehensively are metrics, signals, and thresholds defined and active across all production AI systems — including model inference, training jobs, and data pipelines?
How well is centralised audit logging in place — covering inference activity, access events, and system changes — to support investigation and compliance?
To what extent are agent and RAG workflow traces captured — enabling visibility into multi-step AI actions and retrieval behaviour?
How consistently are monitoring results, system performance summaries, and identified risks communicated to relevant stakeholders — executives, governance leads, and operational teams?
How openly are AI system limitations, known failure modes, and observability gaps shared with the teams and individuals who depend on those systems?
How well does your observability tooling work across different teams, model types, and infrastructure — can all teams access a consistent shared view of AI system health?
How well does your monitoring integrate with governance, risk, and platform systems — do observability signals flow automatically into downstream processes?
To what extent do observability outputs directly influence operational decisions — do monitoring signals result in concrete actions rather than reports that are reviewed but not acted on?
How regularly are observability metrics and measurement frameworks updated to reflect changes in operating environment, stakeholder needs, and emerging risk patterns?
How effectively do your observability outputs inform AI use case evaluation, prioritisation, and retirement decisions — connecting system performance to business value assessment?
How well are security risks measured within your AI systems — including susceptibility to adversarial attacks, model extraction, and prompt injection?
How well is the observability infrastructure itself secured — are logs, traces, and monitoring data protected against tampering, unauthorised access, or sensitive data exposure?
How well are monitoring depth and alert thresholds calibrated to the specific risk profile of each AI system — do higher-risk systems receive proportionately deeper instrumentation?
How dynamically does your observability framework adapt when AI systems are updated, redeployed, or operating in new contexts — without requiring manual reconfiguration?
How effectively does your observability framework detect unsafe or harmful AI outputs — including outputs that violate guardrails, cause harm, or exceed defined boundaries?
How well are safety-related monitoring signals connected to defined escalation and intervention procedures — do safety alerts trigger a documented, tested response?
How well does ongoing compliance monitoring verify that AI systems continue to adhere to regulatory, policy, and governance requirements post-deployment?
Can you articulate how AI usage in your specific role aligns with the organization's broader "Applied AI" strategy?
To what extent do you feel that AI is being introduced to solve real business problems rather than just following a technical trend?
How much dedicated "learning time" are you officially permitted to spend on AI upskilling and literacy each month?
Is there a clear, accessible budget for individuals to experiment with new AI tools or attend specialized AI training?
If an AI system you developed or managed failed publicly, would the internal culture focus on "who to blame" or "how to fix the system"?
Do you feel psychologically safe to flag potential ethical or safety risks in an AI project, even if it might delay a deadline?
When using AI-generated code or text, how consistently do you apply a "verification ritual" to ensure the output is factually grounded?
Are the ECHO habits (Execute, Context, History, Own) a recognized part of the daily workflow for your team?
How often does your team engage in "Adversarial Thinking" sessions to identify potential biases in your AI models?
Do you feel empowered to challenge an AI's recommendation if you believe it is producing an unfair or biased outcome?
How confident are you in your ability to explain the "Reasoning" behind an AI-driven decision to a non-technical client?
Is there a cultural rejection of "Black Box" solutions—do people actively ask "how does this work?"
How frequently do technical and non-technical teams collaborate on the design of AI systems rather than just the delivery?
Do you feel that the "Trustably" language (CARE, 4E) is understood and used across different departments?
How often are "Lessons Learned" from AI failures shared openly across the organization to prevent repeat mistakes?
Is there a central platform (e.g., Slack, Wiki, Handbook) where employees proactively share AI wins and "hacks"?
Do you feel comfortable openly disclosing when you have used AI to assist in a major deliverable (report, code, email)?
Is there a cultural pride in "Human-AI Collaboration," or is there a tendency to mask AI involvement?
Do individuals in your team take personal responsibility for AI outputs, or is "The AI said so" an accepted excuse for errors?
Are the ANCHOR habits (Awareness, Context, History, Ownership, Responsibility) embedded in your performance reviews?
Who do you believe is ultimately responsible if an AI system you use causes a negative impact on a customer?
Is there a consistent vocabulary used across the company to describe AI concepts (e.g., "Grounding" vs. "Hallucination")?
How easily can knowledge from one AI project be applied to another due to shared cultural standards?
Do you view AI as a "Co-pilot" that enhances your creativity, or as a threat to your professional relevance?
Has the introduction of AI made your daily work more or less fulfilling over the last six months?
When using public AI tools, is the instinct to "protect the data" as strong as the instinct to "get the answer"?
Does the culture celebrate "Human Firewalls"—people who proactively identify data leakage risks in AI prompts?
How well do you understand the privacy implications of the specific AI tools you use daily?
How often do you consciously decide not to use AI for a task because you've judged that human intervention is more appropriate?
Is there a cultural understanding of the "High Stakes" vs "Low Stakes" AI use cases within the business?
Is "Safety" viewed as a core professional value within your team, similar to "Quality" or "Speed"?
Are safety guardrails viewed as "helpful protections" or as "annoying obstacles" to be bypassed?
Does the organization reward employees for identifying safety vulnerabilities in AI systems?
How often are AI "Simulations" or "Wargames" held to prepare teams for potential safety incidents?
Is there a moral imperative felt within the team to ensure that AI does no harm to the end user?
Is there a centralized "Service Catalog" or platform roadmap that governs which AI tools and models are approved for use?
To what extent are platform decisions (e.g., choice of Vector DB or LLM provider) driven by long-term organizational strategy rather than ad-hoc project needs?
Are AI infrastructure costs (compute, tokens, storage) automatically attributed to specific projects or business units?
Does the platform provide automated "stopping conditions" or budget caps for long-running agentic or training tasks?
How often is the "Cost-per-Inference" reviewed and optimized through model quantization or rightsizing?
Are all AI infrastructure components (including model endpoints) deployed using Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) for rapid recreation?
Do you have an automated "Rollback" capability for models that exhibit performance degradation post-deployment?
Is your AI stack architected for High Availability (HA) across multiple cloud availability zones?
Is there an automated pipeline that validates model artifacts and data schemas before they reach the production registry?
Does the platform enforce the use of "Certified" base models and base images to ensure supply chain integrity?
Are there standardized "DataOps" tools within the platform for detecting and cleaning anomalies in training or fine-tuning datasets?
Does the platform provide shared libraries or templates for quantitative fairness testing?
Is the full lineage—from raw data source to model version to specific prompt—automatically captured and stored?
Can you programmatically retrieve the specific "System Prompt" and configuration used for any historical inference?
How seamlessly do your data registries, model stores, and application layers communicate via standardized APIs?
Can model endpoints be swapped (e.g., GPT-4 to Llama-3) without requiring significant code changes in the application layer?
Does the platform provide a centralized dashboard for monitoring resource saturation (GPU/CPU) across all AI workloads?
Is there automated telemetry for tracking the latency and throughput of RAG retrievals specifically?
Are platform limitations (rate limits, quotas, and "Known Issues") visible to all engineering teams via a live status page?
Do you provide internal stakeholders with periodic "Consumption Reports" that detail performance vs. spend?
Are there formally defined Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for platform uptime and model API response times?
Is there a clear escalation path for when an automated safety guardrail triggers a system-wide block?
Does the platform support multi-cloud or hybrid deployment patterns for data residency requirements?
Are prompt templates and vector embeddings stored in formats that are portable across different vector databases or LLM providers?
Can a new engineer provision a fully-governed AI sandbox environment in under 30 minutes?
Does the platform provide a "Self-Service" portal for accessing internal datasets and pre-trained models?
Is a Zero-Trust architecture applied, ensuring all model-to-data communications are authenticated and encrypted?
Does the platform automatically scan for "Prompt Injection" vulnerabilities or PII leakage in ingestion pipelines?
Are "Secrets" (API keys, DB credentials) managed via a centralized, hardened vault rather than environment variables?
Does the infrastructure automatically scale or harden security based on the "Risk Tier" assigned to the application?
Can the platform dynamically route requests to different models based on the intent or sensitivity of the input context?
Are there "Circuit Breakers" in place to prevent infinite loops in agentic or multi-step AI workflows?
Does the platform enforce "Egress Controls" to prevent AI agents from calling unapproved external URLs?
Is there a tested "Kill Switch" that can isolate specific models without bringing down the entire platform?
How frequently are "Red Team" simulations run against the platform's safety guardrails?
Is there a documented AI Security Policy that specifically addresses risks like prompt injection, model inversion, and data poisoning?
To what extent is security integrated into the initial "Discovery" phase of new AI use cases rather than as a final gate?
Is there a dedicated budget or resource allocation for recurring AI-specific "Red Teaming" and adversarial testing?
How automated are your security compliance checks across the AI lifecycle to ensure sustainability as the number of models grows?
Is there a tested Incident Response (IR) plan specifically tailored to AI-related breaches (e.g., unauthorized model weight extraction)?
In the event of a compromised AI agent, does the platform have the technical capability to isolate that specific agent without a total system outage?
Are model artifacts, prompts, and vector embeddings backed up in a way that allows for "State Recovery" after a security event?
Are all AI-related code commits and model artifacts digitally signed and verified through a secure supply chain pipeline?
Do you perform automated "Adversarial Robustness" testing on models before they are promoted to production?
Do you periodically audit security logs to ensure that automated filtering/blocking is not disproportionately affecting specific user groups?
Are the "Trust Scores" or risk thresholds used for automated blocking reviewed for quantitative fairness?
When an AI request is blocked or intercepted by a security gate, is the specific policy violation logged with high-fidelity rationale?
Can security analysts reconstruct the full "Chain of Thought" or input/output trace for a flagged security event?
Are AI security events (e.g., suspected prompt injection) automatically fed into the enterprise SIEM/SOAR for centralized alerting?
To what degree are security guardrails "hard-coded" into the platform templates rather than left to individual developers?
Is there real-time monitoring specifically designed to detect "Low and Slow" data exfiltration via multiple small AI queries?
Do you have automated alerts for anomalous model behavior that might indicate a compromised system or unauthorized fine-tuning?
Is there a live dashboard or reporting mechanism that provides stakeholders with the current "Threat Level" for AI assets?
Are vulnerabilities in third-party AI libraries (e.g., LangChain, PyTorch) tracked and reported transparently to engineering teams?
Does the Security Lead have the formal "Stop-Work" authority to deactivate a production model if a critical vulnerability is found?
Are individual developers held accountable for security through automated "Security Scorecards" for their AI applications?
Are security policies (e.g., PII filtering rules) managed centrally such that they apply regardless of which model or cloud is used?
Can security telemetry be shared between different components of the AI stack (e.g., from the WAF to the Vector DB)?
Does the security framework provide "Fast-Track" approvals for use cases that utilize pre-approved, hardened templates?
How much friction do developers report when trying to integrate security guardrails into their local development environment?
Is a Zero-Trust model enforced for all agentic workflows, requiring re-authentication for every external tool call?
Is PII detection and redaction operational for both the input (prompts) and the output (completions)?
Are all sensitive data assets stored in "Silos" with identity-based access controls (DASF Component 1)?
Do you use "Honeypot" prompts or data to detect model extraction or scraping attempts?
Does the security posture (e.g., depth of filtering) automatically increase when a model is exposed to an external, untrusted network?
Are user "Trust Scores" dynamically adjusted based on the intent and sensitivity of their historical AI interactions?
Are there automated guardrails (e.g., LlamaGuard) that scan completions for harmful, illegal, or out-of-boundary content?
Is "Rate Limiting" applied at the user and application level to prevent Denial of Service (DoS) via expensive model calls?
Do you have a "Human-in-the-loop" requirement for high-impact AI actions (e.g., executing a bank transfer or deleting data)?
Are egress controls (firewalls) restricted to a "White List" of approved URLs for all agentic AI workloads?
